content.body: to be honest, I was only thinking about a consumption-oriented bundle, but you're right. I think this detail will hinder export-side adoption, because it's basically asking library authors to abandon lots of ergonomic conveniences (`extends`, private elements, etc.)
content.formatted_body: to be honest, I was only thinking about a consumption-oriented bundle, but you're right. I think this detail will hinder export-side adoption, because it's basically asking library authors to abandon lots of ergonomic conveniences (extends, private elements, etc.)

content.body: Something like https://github.com/tc39/proposal-private-declarations will likely be needed to help on the producer side
content.formatted_body: Something like https://github.com/tc39/proposal-private-declarations will likely be needed to help on the producer side

content.body: extends is maybe less of an issue as pipeline already means the consumer is bypassing any dynamic dispatch
content.formatted_body: extends is maybe less of an issue as pipeline already means the consumer is bypassing any dynamic dispatch

content.body: but it still means manually propagating methods associated with the parent

to achieve reliable DCE the connection between declaration and usage needs to be static.
is there an alternative?
content.formatted_body:
to achieve reliable DCE the connection between declaration and usage needs to be static.
is there an alternative?

extends doesn't disrupt such connections, nor even the ability to observe them. This is in part about the gap between code that static analysis can classify as unused vs. code that current static analysis does classify as unused, and also about interplay between authors, tools, and the language itself. As a demonstration, consider expanding the example from above:

export class C {
  /* constructor and "core" members */
  #counter = 0n;
  constructor(initial = 0n) { this.#counter = validateBigInt(initial); }
  adjustBy(n) { this.#counter += validateBigInt(n); }
  get counter() { return this.#counter; }
};
const { prototype: CShakable } = class extends C {
  /* tree-shakable methods */
  increment() { this.adjustBy(1n); return this.counter; }
  decrement() { this.adjustBy(-1n); return this.counter; }
};
export const { increment, decrement } = CShakable;

import { C, increment } from "c.js";
console.log(increment.call(new C(99n))); // 100n

m.new_content.formatted_body:
extends doesn't disrupt such connections, nor even the ability to observe them. This is in part about the gap between code that static analysis can classify as unused vs. code that current static analysis does classify as unused, and also about interplay between authors, tools, and the language itself. As a demonstration, consider expanding the example from above:

export class C {
  /* constructor and "core" members */
  #counter = 0n;
  constructor(initial = 0n) { this.#counter = BigInt(initial); }
  adjustBy(n) { this.#counter += BigInt(n); }
  get counter() { return this.#counter; }
};
const { prototype: CShakable } = class extends C {
  /* tree-shakable methods */
  increment() { this.adjustBy(1n); return this.counter; }
  decrement() { this.adjustBy(-1n); return this.counter; }
};
export const { increment, decrement } = CShakable;

import { C, increment } from "c.js";
console.log(increment.call(new C(99n))); // 100n

content.body:
This is similar to CJS. Some CJS patterns were statically analysable - and in some tools more than others.
ESM syntax was great for giving tools a standard to follow.
the above could be re-written in so many different ways

```
export const { prototype: { increment, decrement } } = class ...
```

content.formatted_body:
or

class Methods extends C { ... }
export const { increment, decrement } = C.prototype;

m.new_content.formatted_body:
or

class Methods extends C { ... }
export const { increment, decrement } = Methods.prototype;

content.body:
My first outreach attempt (Three.js): https://discourse.threejs.org/t/im-on-tc39-would-three-js-be-interested-in-tree-shakable-methods-syntax/90519

My takeaways:

1. Don't reply more than once per week
2. Ignore replies that consist of off-topic ratholes and **feelings**
3. Tweak OP to address common questions/misunderstandings

Feel free to judge my attempt mercilessly so I can do better next time. Thx :)
